text: The Stoic school distinguished between three types of content competing for the mind's attention: necessary impressions that support action, unnecessary impressions that merely entertain, and corrupting impressions that actively distort judgment. Cleanthes, who succeeded Zeno as head of the Stoa, used the metaphor of a wax tablet: the mind only has so much surface, and every idle impression pressed into it displaces the capacity for clear inscription. This is not asceticism but information hygiene — an argument that the quality of one's thinking depends not only on what one attends to, but on what one refuses to attend to.
question: What was Cleanthes' wax tablet metaphor meant to illustrate?

options.text: That idle impressions displace capacity for clear thought by filling limited mental space
That memories fade over time and must be rehearsed daily
That only written philosophy survives the corruption of oral tradition
That the mind becomes sharper the more impressions it processes
